description: The provided low-resolution blurred image shows a user interface button marked with the text 'Check for updates', a two-tone blue and yellow circular ring, and a red bookmark-like icon at the center. No materials science-related content relevant to the Fe-Mn-C-Al system low-density steel review, including no micrographs, diffraction spectra, performance test curves, phase diagrams, processing flow charts, or structure models, is identifiable in this image. No information related to the alloy composition design, preparation processes, microstructure evolution, strengthening mechanisms, or service performance of the Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels discussed in the review can be extracted from this visual element.

This figure presents three equilibrium phase diagrams of Fe-C based alloy systems to reveal the effects of Mn and Al addition on phase constituents and phase regions. Subplot (a) is the binary Fe-C phase diagram, with x-axis representing C content (0-4.0 wt.%) and y-axis representing temperature (200-1600 °C), covering phase regions including liquid (L), δ-ferrite, γ-austenite, α-ferrite, and cementite (M3C). Subplot (b) shows the Fe-15Mn-C ternary phase diagram, where Mn addition stabilizes austenite, introduces additional carbides (M23C6, M7C3) and multi-phase regions such as α+γ+M3C. Subplot (c) illustrates the Fe-7Al-C ternary phase diagram, with Al addition leading to the formation of κ-carbide and ordered B2 phase, the dashed line denotes the A2 to B2 transition temperature. The comparison demonstrates that alloying with Mn and Al significantly modifies phase equilibrium, phase region ranges and phase types in Fe-C system, providing fundamental thermodynamic guidance for composition design and heat treatment process optimization of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels.

This set of figures demonstrates the distribution of κ-carbide at different precipitation positions in Fe-Mn-Al-C system low-density steels. Subfigures (a, b) present κ*-carbide precipitated along grain boundaries: (a) is a low-magnification scanning electron microscopy image with a 20 μm scale bar, showing α-ferrite and intergranular κ-carbide; (b) is a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image with a 0.5 μm scale bar, coupled with a selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern inset for κ-carbide phase identification. Subfigures (c, d) show intragranular κ'-carbide: (c) is a scanning electron microscopy image with a 4 μm scale bar, revealing lamellar γ-austenite, α-ferrite and κ-carbide; (d) is a TEM image with a 1 μm scale bar showing γ twins, with a SAED pattern inset for calibration. Subfigures (e, f) display κ-carbide precipitated both along grain boundaries and within grains: (e) is a TEM image with a 0.5 μm scale bar presenting alternating α, γ and κ phases; (f) is a SAED pattern with a 5 1/nm scale bar, calibrating the crystallographic orientation of κ-carbide to confirm its ordered structure. This figure verifies three existing precipitation modes of κ-carbide, providing morphological and structural evidence for understanding the precipitation strengthening mechanism of the steel.

This figure presents a temperature-time schematic of thermomechanical processing routes for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, with time as the horizontal axis and temperature as the vertical axis. The general initial processing steps include 1100-1200 °C solution treatment, followed by hot rolling, then cold rolling. Four post-cold-rolling processing routes are illustrated: Route 1 refers to annealing at 600-800 °C before cooling, suitable for austenite-based steels after rapid cooling from hot rolling; Route 2 refers to 1100-1200 °C annealing followed by cooling, used to tailor δ-ferrite size, texture and κ-carbide; Routes 3 and 4 involve aging at 450-700 °C with different cooling paths, which promote nano-precipitate nucleation to achieve precipitation strengthening. This schematic summarizes typical processing paths to obtain targeted microstructures and desired mechanical properties for Fe-Mn-Al-C steels.

This plot presents the Vickers hardness evolution of Fe-22.5Mn-1.2Al-0.3C low-density steel as a function of annealing time under different cold rolling and annealing conditions. Three curves are included: black squares represent samples with 30% cold rolling reduction annealed at 630 °C, black circles represent samples with 40% cold rolling reduction annealed at 550 °C, black triangles represent samples with 50% cold rolling reduction annealed at 550 °C. The x-axis is annealing time in second with a logarithmic scale ranging from 0.1 s to 100000 s, and the y-axis is Vickers hardness (HV) ranging from 150 HV to 450 HV. Initial hardness increases with higher cold rolling reduction, starting at ~335 HV for 30% reduction, ~395 HV for 40% reduction, and ~425 HV for 50% reduction. For all samples, hardness decreases with increasing annealing time, with an obvious accelerated drop at longer annealing durations, which is attributed to recovery and recrystallization eliminating work hardening. This figure supports the conclusion that higher cold rolling reduction introduces stronger work hardening to improve hardness, while annealing induced recrystallization reduces hardness, providing reference for mechanical property regulation of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels via cold rolling and annealing process design.

This figure consists of two subplots demonstrating mechanical property related rules for austenite steels, targeting Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel systems. Subplot (a) plots the yield strength increment (unit: MPa) against alloying element content (unit: wt.%): interstitial elements N and C deliver the most prominent solid solution strengthening effect, followed by Mo, V, Si, while Mn introduces negligible strengthening, and Ni slightly lowers yield strength, confirming that interstitial atoms produce far stronger hardening effect than substitutional atoms. Subplot (b) presents engineering stress-strain curves of samples with varied austenite grain sizes (1.12 μm, 1.48 μm, 1.75 μm, 20.70 μm): finer austenite grains correlate to higher yield strength and ultimate tensile strength, which conforms to the Hall-Petch effect, and all samples retain favorable plasticity before necking occurs. The figure collectively supports that interstitial solid solution strengthening and grain refinement are two effective strengthening mechanisms for austenitic low-density steels.

This figure consists of four subfigures focusing on B2 ordered phases in Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigures (a) and (b) are crystal structure models of CsCl-type B2 phases: (a) shows (Fe,Mn)Al-type B2 lattice with Fe/Mn atoms at cube vertices, Al at body center, and interstitial C atoms; (b) shows NiAl-type B2 lattice with Ni at cube vertices and Al at body center. Subfigure (c) is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image presenting three B2 morphologies in aged Fe-16Mn-9.6Al-0.86C-4.9Ni steel: banded (region 1), blocky (region 2), and ultrafine (region 3), with a 10 μm scale bar. Subfigure (d) is a low-magnification micrograph (500 μm scale bar, inset with same scale) showing B2 precipitates in austenite (γ) matrix. This figure confirms that B2 phases are key strengthening precipitates: properly distributed B2 improves strength and work hardening, while excessive intergranular brittle B2 degrades plasticity and toughness.

This 3D plot illustrates the relationship between chemical composition, deformation mechanisms and stacking fault energy (SFE) of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels at room temperature. The three axes represent C content (0-1.2 wt.%), Mn content (0-35 wt.%), and Al content (0-12 wt.%), respectively. It marks distinct regions corresponding to different deformation mechanisms and phase constitutions: TRIP, TWIP, MBIP, MBIP + Kappa carbide, TWIP + Kappa carbide, as well as the kappa-carbide forming border. Two iso-SFE contours for 20 mJ/m² and 50 mJ/m² are also presented, with the color bar mapping SFE values from 0 to 1.2. The figure demonstrates that Al, Mn and C contents regulate SFE and κ-carbide precipitation, thus determining the dominant deformation mechanism of the steel, which supports the composition design principle for Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels to achieve targeted mechanical properties.

This set of TEM characterizations reveals the deformation twin formation mechanism near grain boundaries in Fe-17.5Mn-1.5Al-0.58C TWIP steel under different tensile strains. Subfigures (a)-(c) present TEM micrographs of samples strained to 0.02, with red arrows marking stacking faults and orange arrows indicating Frank partial dislocations on (111) planes, accompanied by selected area electron diffraction patterns with g=(111) and g=(311) respectively, scale bars are 100 nm, 100 nm, 50 nm for (a), (b), (c). Subfigures (d)-(f) show samples strained to 0.05: (d) is low-magnification TEM (scale bar 500 nm) showing the region near grain boundary between austenite Grain A and Grain B; (e) is high-magnification TEM (scale bar 20 nm) with red arrows marking twin nucleation sites and yellow arrow showing twin growth direction; (f) is selected area electron diffraction pattern with zone axis // [011], blue and red diffraction lattices correspond to Grain A matrix and deformation twin respectively, confirming the twin orientation relationship. The figure supports the conclusion that austenite grain boundaries act as preferred nucleation sites for deformation twins: at strain 0.02, dislocation interactions form stacking faults and partial dislocations near grain boundaries; when strain increases to 0.05, deformation twins nucleate at grain boundaries and grow into grain interiors, both dislocation motion and deformation twins contribute to the high strain hardening of TWIP steels.

This set of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images illustrates the evolution of dislocation substructures in Fe-20Mn-9Al-0.6C duplex phase steel under different tensile strains. Subfigures (a)-(d) present austenite microstructures at 5%, 10%, 20%, and 40% tensile strain respectively: unidirectional slip on the (1$ ar{1}$1) plane is observed at 5% strain, multidirectional slips on both (1$ ar{1}$1) and (11$ ar{1}$) planes occur when strain rises to 10% and 20%, and unidirectional slip with significantly smaller slip line spacing appears at 40% strain. Subfigures (e) and (f) show ferrite microstructures at 5% and 40% strain: dislocation motion dominates deformation at 5% low strain, while explosive dislocation multiplication generates dislocation cells at 40% high strain. These findings confirm that austenite follows a planar slip mechanism due to high stacking fault energy, while ferrite exhibits wavy slip and forms dislocation cells during deformation, supporting the distinct strain hardening contributions of the two phases in this low-density steel system.

This figure presents the strain hardening rate plot of Fe-12Mn-8Al-0.8C austenite-based duplex phase (A-BDP) low-density steel, with the x-axis representing lnσ (MPa) and the y-axis representing ln(dσ/dε) (MPa). The curve is divided into three distinct stages separated by critical strains ε₁ = 0.05 and ε₂ = 0.30. In Stage 1 (strain < 0.05), single ferrite deformation leads to a sharp decrease in strain hardening rate. In Stage 2 (strain between 0.05 and 0.30), austenite starts to participate in deformation, so the curve shows an approximately linear slow downward trend. In Stage 3 (strain > 0.30), severe strain localization caused by the hardness difference between ferrite and austenite results in a rapid drop of strain hardening rate. This figure confirms the multi-stage strain hardening behavior of A-BDP steel derived from the hardness difference between constituent phases, supporting the deformation mechanism analysis of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels.

This set of 6 TEM micrographs and corresponding selected area electron diffraction patterns characterize the microstructure of as quenched-annealed Fe-27Mn-12Al-0.8C duplex low-density steel before and after 5% tensile deformation. Subfigure (a) presents fine DO₃ precipitates in ferrite with its matching SAED pattern inset. Subfigure (b) shows nano-sized κ-carbide precipitates in austenite with corresponding SAED pattern inset. Subfigures (c) and (d) display the deformed ferrite microstructure after 5% strain, revealing dislocations inside B2 domains. Subfigures (e) and (f) show the deformed austenite microstructure after 5% strain. The observations confirm that κ-carbides in austenite are sheared by plane slip dislocations during deformation, while strong interactions between super-dislocations and ordered phases occur in ferrite, supporting the conclusion that strain hardening of this steel is controlled by both super-dislocation shearing of ordered phases in ferrite and plane slip in austenite.

This figure illustrates the strain hardening behavior and underlying strengthening mechanisms of Fe-3.9Mn-5.8Al-0.35C ferrite-based duplex phase (F-BDP) low-density steel under tensile deformation. Subplot (a) displays true stress and strain hardening rate curves against true strain, divided into four distinct stages, with y axes in units of MPa for both true stress and strain hardening rate. Subplots (b-d) characterize microstructures at 10% tensile strain: (b) identifies martensite (M) and retained austenite (RA) with a 500 nm scale bar, accompanied by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns for [1̅11] and [011] zone axes (scale bar 5 1/nm); (c) with 200 nm scale shows nanoscale grains, (d) presents deformation twins with matching SAED (200 nm scale). Subplots (e-f) show microstructures at 20% strain: (e) shows deformed phase morphology, (f) marks dislocation arrays (200 nm scale). The TEM results verify that multi-stage strain hardening is regulated by dislocation slip, TRIP effect, and TWIP effect at different deformation stages, supporting the proposed hardening mechanism for F-BDP steels.

This figure consists of two subplots showing Charpy impact absorbed energy of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels as functions of alloying element contents. Subplot (a) illustrates the impact energy of Fe-xMn-2Al-0.45C-0.05Si steel with Mn content ranging from 17 wt.% to 22 wt.%, tested at 0 °C and -196 °C. At 0 °C, the impact energy slightly increases with rising Mn content and stabilizes at ~250 J; at -196 °C, it rises from ~45 J to ~150 J as Mn content increases from 17 to 22 wt.%. Subplot (b) presents the impact energy of Fe-18Mn-xAl-0.6C steel with Al content ranging from 3 wt.% to 8 wt.%; it increases sluggishly from ~120 J to ~138 J as Al content rises from 3 to 5 wt.%, then decreases steeply to ~15 J when Al content reaches 8 wt.%. This figure supports the conclusion that Mn and Al contents significantly influence the cryogenic impact toughness of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels, and optimized element addition is required for good toughness.

This set of figures illustrates the microstructure evolution of Fe-30Mn-6.5Al-0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel during tensile deformation at -196 °C. Subfigure (a) is the microscopic morphology map with a 2 μm scale bar, marking microbands (MB1, MB2, MB3) and deformation twins (TW), attached with a crystal orientation triangle of {001}, {101}, {111} planes. Subfigure (b) is the corresponding strain/defect distribution map with a 2 μm scale bar, matching the microstructures in (a). Subfigure (c) is the EBSD orientation imaging map with a 5 μm scale bar, showing grains G1, G2, different types of twins (TW1, TW2), and the region with dense dislocation walls + microbands (DDWs + MBs), attached with an orientation color scale triangle. Subfigure (d) is the corresponding local strain distribution map with a 5 μm scale bar, using a color scale ranging from 0 (minimum) to 2.5 (maximum) to indicate strain distribution. The results demonstrate that microbands form via planar slip localization under low strain, while new microbands and deformation twins generate simultaneously under high strain; microbands inside austenite grains suppress the formation of deformation twins. This complex deformation structure leads to highly heterogeneous deformation, which significantly enhances work hardening and mechanical properties of the studied steel under cryogenic conditions.

This group of plots presents impact mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subplot (a) shows the relationship between Charpy impact absorbed energy (unit: J) and testing temperature (ranging from -200 °C to 100 °C) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenite steel, compared with conventional austenite stainless steel, dual phase steel, ferrite steel and martensite steel. The impact absorbed energy of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenite steel increases continuously with rising temperature, exhibiting much better cryogenic toughness than other comparative steel grades. Subplot (b) displays the correlation between impact toughness (unit: J/cm²) and Al content (ranging from 3 wt.% to ~9.5 wt.%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel: under two test conditions, impact toughness first increases and then decreases with Al content, reaching a peak at 6-7 wt.% Al. This trend is regulated by κ-carbide precipitation and δ-ferrite formation, indicating Al content is a key factor tuning impact toughness of the steel system.

This figure presents fatigue-induced surface and microstructural features of two Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels. Subfigure (a) is an atomic force microscopy (AFM) 3D topography of the fatigued surface of Fe-16Mn-2Al-0.3C steel, with a 20 μm × 20 μm scan area and 0–200 nm height range. Subfigure (b) shows slip bands in the same fatigued Fe-16Mn-2Al-0.3C steel, with a 2 μm scale bar. Subfigures (c), (d), (e) are transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of deformed microstructures in micro-alloyed Fe-28Mn-7Al-1C steel after fatigue failure: (c) displays dislocation pile-up at 0.4% strain, (d) shows slip traces at 0.4% strain, (e) presents stacking faults at 0.8% strain, all with 0.5 μm scale bars. The figure supports that fatigue performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C steels is closely associated with their composition and cyclic deformation mechanisms, including slip band formation, dislocation accumulation, and stacking fault generation, which contribute to improved fatigue life for micro-alloyed grades.

This set of figures demonstrates hydrogen-induced intergranular crack in Fe-26Mn-11Al-1.2C austenitic steel containing κ-carbides. Subfigure (a) is an EBSD inverse pole figure (IPF) with a 50 μm scale bar, presenting grain orientation distribution, with directions of TD (transverse direction), RD/TA (rolling direction), and ND (normal direction) marked. Subfigure (b) is a kernel average misorientation (KAM) map with a 50 μm scale bar, showing strain distribution around the crack: blue represents 0 strain, while red represents strain of 4~5, confirming apparent strain localization near crack boundaries, the red dashed box marks the region corresponding to subfigure (c). Subfigure (c) is an orientation-optimized electron channeling contrast (ECC) SEM image with a 5 μm scale bar, revealing micro-voids, grain boundaries and strain localization features at the crack tip at higher magnification. The figure supports the conclusion that grain boundary triple junction cracking and slip-localization-induced intergranular cracking along micro-voids dominate the hydrogen embrittlement performance of the steel, and slip-localization-assisted crack growth leads to macroscopic brittle fracture appearance.

This figure presents the microstructure and wear resistance of Fe-25.1Mn-6.6Al-1.3C low-density steel after quenching at 1050 °C and subsequent aging treatment. Subfigures (a-d) are labeled with 500 °C aging for 2 h or 4 h, while the caption and citation report the aging temperature as 550 °C. (a) and (b) are low-magnification micrographs (scale bar 25 μm) of samples aged for 2 h and 4 h respectively: the 2 h aged sample has fine coherent nano-sized κ-carbide precipitated in austenite matrix, while the 4 h aged sample shows coarsened κ-carbide distributed in intragranular regions and grain boundaries. (c) and (d) are high-magnification TEM images (scale bar 0.5 μm) of 2 h and 4 h aged samples, where high-density dislocation tangles and dislocation walls are observed, with no martensite or deformation twins. (e) is a bar chart of wear resistance: the value increases from 1.16 g⁻¹ (as quenched) to the peak of 2.66 g⁻¹ after 2 h aging, then decreases to 2.25 g⁻¹ (3 h) and 1.92 g⁻¹ (4 h). The results confirm that appropriate aging time (2 h) delivers optimal wear resistance, while over-aging leads to carbide coarsening, increased brittleness and reduced wear resistance.

This plot summarizes the room-temperature tensile properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels with varying Mn contents, with x-axis representing yield/tensile strength in MPa and y-axis representing total elongation in %. Steels with different phase constitutions (single austenite phase, single ferrite phase, ferrite-austenite-MC/κ, austenite-based duplex, ferrite-based duplex) are separated by different colored dashed envelopes. Mn contents are categorized into four ranges: 12~18 wt.% (red triangles), 18~25 wt.% (purple squares), 25~28 wt.% (green triangles), and 28~33 wt.% (orange hexagons). Three reference lines of product of strength and elongation (30, 50, 70 GPa·%) are also plotted. It is observed that single austenitic steels generally have the highest strength-elongation product, exceeding 70 GPa·% with maximum total elongation close to 100%, while ferrite-based duplex steels show higher strength but lower plasticity, with strength-elongation product mostly around 30 GPa·%. The core conclusion supported is that the tensile performance of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels is strongly dependent on phase constitution and Mn content, and single austenitic steels achieve the optimal strength-plasticity synergy.
